# Interfaces Outlive Implementations

## The Physics

Implementations are replaced regularly; interfaces accumulate consumers who
depend on them. A database engine can be swapped if the query interface is
preserved. A network service can be rewritten if the API contract is honored.
But changing an interface requires every consumer to adapt — and the cost of
that adaptation grows linearly (or worse) with the number of consumers.

This asymmetry has a ratchet effect. Early in a system's life, interfaces and
implementations are equally cheap to change because there are few consumers.
As adoption grows, the implementation remains replaceable but the interface
becomes increasingly expensive to modify. Eventually the interface becomes
the most durable artifact in the system — outliving every implementation that
has ever served it.

The practical consequence is that interface design decisions have a longer
half-life than any other engineering decision. A poorly chosen interface
persists not because it is good, but because the cost of migration exceeds
the cost of living with the design. This makes interface design the
highest-leverage activity in systems engineering — not because it is
glamorous, but because it is irreversible at scale.

## Why This Matters

- **Investment allocation**: Interface design deserves disproportionate review time relative to implementation, because its consequences persist longer.
- **Versioning strategy**: Interfaces must be versioned and evolved, not replaced, because consumers cannot all migrate simultaneously.
- **Dependency governance**: The number of consumers on an interface determines its change cost — this must be tracked.
- **Technology lifecycle**: When planning technology transitions, the interface layer is the binding constraint, not the implementation.

## Observable Patterns

- APIs that were "temporary" five years ago are still in production because consumers depend on them.
- The most painful migrations are interface migrations, not implementation swaps.
- Systems with stable, well-defined interfaces tolerate multiple implementation replacements over their lifetime.
- Backward-incompatible interface changes produce cascading failures across consumer teams, even when the new interface is objectively superior.

## 1970/2070 Note

The S/360 instruction set architecture from 1964 outlived every hardware
implementation IBM built for it. A 2070 quantum cloud provider's measurement
API will outlive every generation of physical qubits behind it. Interfaces are
the fossils of engineering — they persist long after the organism that
produced them has been replaced.

## Relationship to Meta Layer

- JOS-P14 (Explicit Boundaries) — boundaries are defined by interfaces, and interface longevity makes boundary design critical
- JOS-S10 (Dependency Governance) — dependency cost is determined by interface coupling, not implementation coupling
- JOS-S13 (Technology Lifecycle) — lifecycle planning must account for the asymmetry between interface and implementation durability

### Framework Connection

Framework §8 (boundary contracts as durable interfaces) and §13 (four-layer identity model) reinforce interface longevity — the five-contract boundary model creates stable interfaces that persist across implementation changes, and the identity/resolver layer ensures interface addresses survive refactoring.
